content.problemStatement:
You are given a string SSS consisting of NNN digits from 000 to 999. You want to obtain a palindrome from SSS using some operations.

There are MMM different types of operations you can make on SSS. Each operation is described by three integers  XXX, YYY and WWW. In this operation, if there is a digit equal to XXX in SSS, you can replace XXX with YYY using WWW coins. For example, if X=4,Y=5,W=10X = 4, Y= 5, W = 10X=4,Y=5,W=10 and S=1448S = 1448S=1448, you make SSS equal to either 145814581458 or 154815481548 using 101010 coins.

You can do the operations in any order. One type of operation can be done multiple times. You can use at most KKK coins total in all operations. Among all the palindromes that can be obtained from SSS, output the lexicographically maximum palindrome.

If two strings AAA and BBB have the same length, we say that AAA is lexicographically larger than BBB if there exists an index iii so that A1=B1,A2=B2,‚Ä¶,Ai‚ąí1=Bi‚ąí1,Ai>BiA_1=B_1,A_2=B_2,\ldots,A_{i-1}=B_{i-1}, A_i>B_iA1‚Äč=B1‚Äč,A2‚Äč=B2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,Ai‚ąí1‚Äč=Bi‚ąí1‚Äč,Ai‚Äč>Bi‚Äč.

Print ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1 if it is impossible to obtain a palindrome from SSS using at most KKK coins.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains three integers NNN, MMM, and KKK - the length of the string, the number of operation types, and the maximum number of coins to spend, respectively.
The second line contains a string SSS of length NNN.
Each of the next MMM lines contains three integers XiX_iXi‚Äč, YiY_iYi‚Äč and WiW_iWi‚Äč - the parameters of the iii-th type of operation.


Output Format
For each test case, output the lexicographically maximum palindrome which can be obtained from the given string SSS using at most KKK coins. Print `-1` if it is impossible to obtain a palindrome.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
In the first test case, we convert 777 and 333 into 999 using two and six operations, respectively, 444 into 555 using one operation, and 666 to 777 using one operation. There is no way to obtain a palindrome which is lexicographically larger than 957599575995759 using ten operations.
In the second test case, there is no possible way to convert 111 and 555 into equal digits. So we can't obtain a palindrome.
In the third test case, we convert the 666 in the last index of the given string into 111 using the 444-th type of operation. So SSS becomes 141741141741141741. Next, we convert 111 in the third index into 555 using the 111-st type of operation. So SSS becomes 145741145741145741. Then we convert the 555 in the third index of SSS into 777 using the 222-nd type of operation. So SSS becomes 147741147741147741. The total cost is 5+3+2=105 + 3 + 2 = 105+3+2=10 coins and the string we obtain after all operations is a palindrome. We can't obtain a lexicographically larger string than 147741147741147741 using at most 121212 coins.
